results.seo_title: Nvidia and EPRI Build Micro Data Centers Near Utility Substations…
results.meta_description: Nvidia and EPRI are building micro data centers near utility substations to improve AI flexibility and reduce power consumption during peak demand.
results.body_html:
The companies are building a fleet of small data centers right next to substations to improve AI flexibility and reduce power consumption during peak demand.

Reduce power consumption during peak demand by shifting compute workloads to nearby substations with spare capacity Improve data center flexibility and accommodate electricity availability Decrease the need for new grid infrastructure, such as power lines and poles, and upgraded transformers and switch gear

Technical Details of Distributed Inference

AI TrainingTightly interconnected GPUs, high-speed links (e.g., NVLink and InfiniBand interconnects)

InferenceSmaller data centers with fewer GPUs and less fancy networking

Challenges and Opportunities Nvidia and EPRI are exploring ways to reduce power consumption during peak demand, which could lead to increased energy flexibility and reduced costs for data center operators.

The project aims to begin construction of the pilot fleet of small data centers by the end of 2026 Nvidia and EPRI estimate that compute workloads will need to be moved to a different substation only about 0.1 percent of the time

Site selection and permitting Design and construction of small data centers near substations Testing and validation of distributed inference capabilities Pilot project deployment and evaluation

source.title: Your Next AI Query May Travel Where the Power Is
source.summary:
The rise of electricity-guzzling data centers has forced the artificial intelligence industry to get creative about finding power. One of the latest ideas: Build micro data centers next to utility substations and operate them in concert, shifting the computation around based on power availability.

That’s the approach Nvidia and its collaborators are taking in a new pilot project they plan to build later this year. They’ll construct about 25 of these small data centers, each ranging from 5 to 20 megawatts, across five utilities in the United States. If one substation is overloaded with power demand, or if there’s an outage, the compute will be shifted to a different data center near a substation that has spare capacity.

To develop the fleet, Nvidia is partnering with data center builder InfraPartners, real estate service provider Prologis, and the nonprofit EPRI (formerly known as the Electric Power Research Institute).

The project aims to demonstrate a new way for data centers to be more flexible and accommodating of electricity availability. It’s also a way for data center developers to quickly secure power from the grid—an increasingly precious commodity, even in small chunks.

“We started looking at how much [unused] power is available at individual substations, and what we found was that on average, like 5 MW is nominally available…max 20 MW,” says

results.seo_title: Meta Celebrates African Cinema With 'Made by Africa, Loved by the…
results.meta_description: Meta launches sixth edition of its pan-African campaign, 'Made by Africa, loved by the world: Where stories spark community,' featuring a five-part vodcast…
results.body_html:
Africa Day is celebrated on May 25, and this year, Facebook has launched the sixth edition of its pan-African campaign, ‘Made by Africa, loved by the world: Where stories spark community,’ featuring a five-part vodcast series profiling internationally acclaimed actors and filmmakers from Nigeria and South Africa.

Featured Talents

  • Kehinde Bankole (Nigeria) – Two-time AMVCA winner; performed at Shakespeare’s Globe Theatre; voiced Disney’s Iwaju; starred in Netflix and Amazon Prime productions.
  • Linda Mtoba (South Africa) – Award-winning actress known for Isibaya, The River, and BET’s Queendom; digital entrepreneur and presenter.
  • Nomzamo Mbatha (South Africa) – TIME 100 Next honoree, UNHCR Goodwill Ambassador, and WEF Young Global Leader; starred in Coming 2 America and Shaka iLembe; executive producer and founder of the Nomzamo Lighthouse Foundation.
  • Osas Ighodaro (Nigeria) – Two-time AMVCA Best Actress winner; Nigerian-American actress and producer known for The Smart Money Woman and Fela and the Kalakuta Queens; founder of Imuenyan Productions.
  • Tobi Bakre (Nigeria) – AMVCA-nominated actor known for Brotherhood and Prime Video’s Gangs of Lagos; one of Nollywood’s fastest-rising leading men.

Partnership with Amplify Africa

Africa Day is about more than a moment in history, it’s a celebration of who we are, and the impact African culture continues to have on the world. For ten years, Amplify Africa has been dedicated to telling that story, so partnering with Meta on this Made by Africa Campaign feels like a natural fit.

“Africa Day is about more than a moment in history, it’s a celebration of who we are, and the impact African culture continues to have on the world. For ten years, Amplify Africa has been dedicated to telling that story, so partnering with Meta on this Made by Africa Campaign feels like a natural fit.” – Dami Kujembola, CEO & Co-Founder, Amplify Africa

Community Challenge #ShareYourAfrica

The campaign also evolves its community challenge, #ShareYourAfrica, inviting creators across lifestyle, fashion, and dance to use Meta AI to celebrate African culture through Reels showcasing the traditions and moments shaping African identity today.

PlatformDescription
Meta Africa Facebook pageVodcast snippets will be available on this platform.
Podcasters’ channelsFull episodes of the vodcast series will be available on these platforms.
Talent profilesProfiles of the featured talents will be available on this platform.

source.title: Celebrating African Cinema With the ‘Made by Africa, Loved by the World’ 2026 Campaign
source.summary: In celebration of Africa Day on May 25, Facebook has launched the sixth edition of its pan-African campaign, ‘Made by Africa, loved by the world: Where stories spark community.’ This year’s campaign shifts the spotlight to African cinema.  The 2026 campaign features a five-part vodcast series profiling five internationally acclaimed actors and filmmakers from Nigeria and South Africa, hosted by leading African podcasters I Said What I Said (Nigeria) and Because We Said So (South Africa). “For six years, Made by Africa has spotlighted talent from across the continent making a mark globally. This year, film takes centre stage. From Nollywood to South African cinema, African stories are reaching audiences worldwide, and Facebook is at the heart of how people come together around cultural moments like these. This campaign backs the filmmakers driving that momentum.” said, Kezia Anim-Addo, Communications Director, Africa, Middle East & Turkey The featured talents: Kehinde Bankole (Nigeria) – Two-time AMVCA winner; performed at Shakespeare’s Globe Theatre; voiced Disney’s Iwaju; starred in Netflix and Amazon Prime productions. Linda Mtoba (South Africa) – Award-winning actress known for Isibaya, The River, and BET’s Queendom; digital entrepreneur and presenter. Nomzamo Mbatha (South Africa) – TIME 100 Next honoree, UNHCR Goodwill Ambassador, and WEF Young Global Leader; starred in Coming 2 America and Shaka iLembe; executive producer and founder of the Nomzamo Lighthouse Foundation. Osas Ighodaro (Nigeria) – Two-time AMVCA Best Actress winner; Nigerian-American actress and producer known for The Smart Money Woman and Fela and the Kalakuta Queens; founder of Imuenyan Productions. Tobi Bakre (Nigeria) – AMVCA-nominated actor

results.seo_title: Meta Introduces Completely Private Way to Chat With AI
results.meta_description: Learn about Meta's Incognito Chat feature that allows private conversations with AI, and explore its benefits and limitations.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Meta has introduced a completely private way to chat with AI, allowing users to have invisible conversations.

How Incognito Chat Works

  • Incognito Chat uses end-to-end encryption to ensure that only the user and Meta AI can access the conversation.
  • The feature is available on WhatsApp and other messaging platforms.
  • Users can switch between private and public modes at will.

Benefits of Incognito Chat

FeatureDescription
End-to-end encryptionEnsures that only the user and Meta AI can access the conversation.
Private modeAllows users to have invisible conversations.
Switchable modesUsers can switch between private and public modes at will.

Limitations of Incognito Chat

While Incognito Chat offers several benefits, it also has some limitations. For example:

Incognito Chat is only available on WhatsApp and other messaging platforms.

source.title: Introducing a Completely Private Way to Chat With AI
source.summary:
Incognito Chat lets you talk to Meta AI in a way that is invisible to anyone else.

The post Introducing a Completely Private Way to Chat With AI appeared first on Meta Newsroom.

results.seo_title: Chain-of-Thought Disrupts Simple Steering of Refusal in Large…
results.meta_description: Researchers find that chain-of-thought (CoT) traces in large reasoning models (LRMs) actively reinforce refusal, making them more robust against…
results.body_html:
Researchers have found that chain-of-thought (CoT) traces in large reasoning models (LRMs) actively reinforce refusal, making them more robust against activation-level interventions.

Key Findings

  • Activation steering reverses refusal in only 39% of cases when CoT is kept fixed, but removing CoT increases this to 70%
  • In a two-stage intervention where model regenerates its CoT under activation steering, refusal is reversed in 94% of cases
  • CoT can carry and reconstruct compliance signal independently

Robustness Against Activation-Level Interventions

This joint activation makes LRM more robust against activation-level interventions alone, but exposes CoT to a possible alternative surface attack.

Implications for Unlearning and Safety Evaluation

  • R-TOFU benchmark provides realistic CoT annotations and step-wise metrics that expose residual knowledge invisible to answer-level checks
  • Conventional answer-only objectives leave substantial forget traces in reasoning
target_keywords: Chain-of-Thought Disrupts Simple Steering of Refusal

source.title: Beyond a Single Direction: Chain-of-Thought Disrupts Simple Steering of Refusal
source.summary: Researchers have found that large reasoning models (LRMs) use chain-of-thought (CoT) traces to generate their outputs, which can complicate control mechanisms such as refusal. In one study, removing the CoT entirely increased the effectiveness of activation steering in reversing refusal from 39% to 70%. This suggests that the CoT actively reinforces refusal and can carry and reconstruct the compliance signal independently, making LRMs more robust against certain types of attacks. The findings highlight the importance of considering both residual stream activations and CoT when developing control mechanisms for LRM-based systems.

results.seo_title: InfoQuant: Efficient Large Language Model Quantization for Improved…
results.meta_description: Learn how InfoQuant shapes activation distributions for low-bit LLM quantization, outperforming prior PTQ and end-to-end training baselines.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Low-bit activation quantization remains a major bottleneck in efficient large language model (LLM) deployment.

Key Findings

  • InfoQuant consistently outperforms prior PTQ and end-to-end training baselines across multiple LLM families.
  • Under W4A4KV4, InfoQuant preserves 97% of floating-point accuracy on average.
  • InfoQuant reduces the LLaMA-2 13B performance gap by 42% over the previous state of the art.

Methodology

InfoQuant employs Peak Suppression Orthogonal Transformation (PSOT) to shape activations into more quantization-friendly distributions.

Our analysis shows that quantization-friendly activations should jointly have a smaller numerical range and sufficient dispersion within that range.

Advantages

AdvantageDescription
Improved RobustnessInfoQuant's adaptive outlier-token selection improves the robustness of PSOT during optimization.
Reduced Quantization ErrorInfoQuant preserves 97% of floating-point accuracy on average, reducing the LLaMA-2 13B performance gap by 42%.
Efficient DeploymentInfoQuant enables efficient large language model deployment with low-bit activation quantization.

source.title: InfoQuant: Shaping Activation Distributions for Low-Bit LLM Quantization
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new method called InfoQuant to improve the efficiency of large language models (LLMs) by reducing quantization error during low-bit activation quantization. The method, which is train-free and does not require any additional data, uses Peak Suppression Orthogonal Transformation (PSOT) to shape activations into more quantization-friendly distributions. This approach has been shown to consistently outperform prior methods across multiple LLM families, with an average preservation of 97% of floating-point accuracy on W4A4KV4 and a reduction in the performance gap by 42%.

results.seo_title: Lighthouse Attention: A Training-Only Selection-Based Hierarchical…
results.meta_description: Nous Research Proposes Lighthouse Attention, a selection-based hierarchical attention mechanism that achieves a 1.40–1.69× end-to-end wall-clock speedup…
results.body_html:
Lighthouse Attention achieves a 1.40–1.69× end-to-end wall-clock speedup against a cuDNN SDPA baseline with matching or lower final training loss.

Key Benefits of Lighthouse Attention

Reduces attention call from O(N·S·d) to O(S²·d) Pools Q, K, and V symmetrically across a multi-resolution pyramid Runs stock FlashAttention on a small dense sub-sequence

Lighthouse Attention Performance Benchmarks

Lighthouse Attention Advantages Significantly outperforms existing open-source language models with comparable sizes.

source.title: Nous Research Proposes Lighthouse Attention: A Training-Only Selection-Based Hierarchical Attention That Delivers 1.4–1.7× Pretraining Speedup at Long Context
source.summary:
Nous Research has published Lighthouse Attention, a selection-based hierarchical attention mechanism that wraps around standard scaled dot-product attention during pretraining and is removed afterward. Unlike prior methods such as NSA and HISA that pool only keys and values, Lighthouse pools Q, K, and V symmetrically across a multi-resolution pyramid, reducing the attention call from O(N·S·d) to O(S²·d) and running stock FlashAttention on a small dense sub-sequence. Tested on a 530M Llama-3-style model at 98K context, it achieves a 1.40–1.69× end-to-end wall-clock speedup against a cuDNN SDPA baseline with matching or lower final training loss.

The post Nous Research Proposes Lighthouse Attention: A Training-Only Selection-Based Hierarchical Attention That Delivers 1.4–1.7× Pretraining Speedup at Long Context appeared first on MarkTechPost.

results.seo_title: Hybrid Neural-Symbolic Pipeline Outperforms Generation in Clinical…
results.meta_description: A hybrid neural-symbolic pipeline achieves high accuracy in extracting clinical follow-up instructions from outpatient notes, outperforming generative…
results.body_html:
Direct answer: A hybrid neural-symbolic pipeline achieves high accuracy in extracting clinical follow-up instructions from outpatient notes, outperforming generative extractors.

Methodology

  • BioBERT feeds BIO tagging and a biaffine linker;
  • Entities are canonicalized via a 28-action ontology and times normalized to day offsets deterministically;
  • A hybrid neural-symbolic pipeline is used for extraction.

Results

ModelF1 Score
Hybrid Pipeline0.997
LLaMA-30.992
GPT-4o-mini0.963

Conclusion

The hybrid neural-symbolic pipeline achieves high accuracy in extracting clinical follow-up instructions from outpatient notes, outperforming generative extractors.

source.title: Reliable Extraction of Clinical Follow-Up Instructions: A Hybrid Neural-Symbolic Pipeline
source.summary: Researchers have developed a hybrid neural-symbolic pipeline to extract clinical follow-up instructions from outpatient notes, which pairs actions with future times. The system outperforms generative extractors by separating learned entity extraction from deterministic date arithmetic, achieving high accuracy on a synthetic corpus and generalizing well to unseen actions. The results show that the hybrid approach is more effective than baselines in extracting both action-disjoint and paired instructions.

results.seo_title: Compressive Knowledge Graph Hypothesis: What Matters for Scientific…
results.meta_description: A new hypothesis suggests that useful knowledge graph signal is often recoverable from compact, scientifically structured subgraphs rather than requiring the…
results.body_html:
Direct answer: According to the study, useful knowledge graph signal is often recoverable from compact, scientifically structured subgraphs rather than requiring the full local graph.

Perturbing local KGs by varying density, ontology richness, topology, and control structure changes outputs across models. No-KG outputs also recover substantial graph content from model priors. Compact top-k subgraphs often approximate full-KG behavior, including when claimed-outcome triples are held out.

Mistral 7B vs Llama-3.1-70B MetricMistral 7BLlama-3.1-70B Reasoning, Mathematics, and Code GenerationOutperforms Llama 2 13B across all evaluated benchmarks.Underperforms compared to Mistral 7B.

Robotic AI Chemist Capabilities ChemAgents, a hierarchical multiagent system, enables autonomous chemical research on demand using an on-board Llama-3.1-70B LLM.

Comparative Evaluation of AI Platforms PlatformPerformance Google Gemini 2.5 FlashOutperforms other platforms in solving multiple-choice questions.

source.title: The Compressive Knowledge Graph Hypothesis: Which Graph Facts Matter for Scientific Hypothesis Generation?
source.summary: Researchers have investigated how knowledge graphs (KGs) influence scientific hypothesis generation in language models. They found that the utility of KGs varies depending on the model and context, with some outputs being significantly affected by graph facts while others can recover substantial content from prior knowledge. The study suggests that compact subgraphs within KGs can approximate full-KG behavior, supporting a "Compressive KG Hypothesis" that useful signal is often recoverable from structured subgraphs rather than requiring the entire local graph.

results.seo_title: Accelerating Long-Tail Generation in Synchronous RLHF Training via…
results.meta_description: Discover how PAT reduces generation latency by up to 34.6% and end-to-end RLHF training iteration latency by up to 27.2% compared to the original VeRL setup.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: The proposed PAT method reduces generation latency by up to 34.6% and end-to-end RLHF training iteration latency by up to 27.2% compared to the original VeRL setup.

A predictor-guided online reconfiguration method decides both the reconfiguration point and the target TP configuration based on offline profiling, triggering reconfiguration only when the predicted latency benefit outweighs the reconfiguration overhead. A lightweight online reconfiguration mechanism updates only the states and layouts affected by TP changes: it adapts unfinished decoding states through a cost-model-based choice between KV-cache migration and recomputation, performs in-place weight resharding, and reuses cached communication groups.

ModelOriginal VeRL SetupPAT Setup LLaMA3.1-8B34.6%27.2% Qwen3-14B34.6%27.2%

Benchmarks and Gaps DeepScaleR shows that PAT reduces generation latency by up to 34.6% and end-to-end RLHF training iteration latency by up to 27.2% compared to the original VeRL setup.

source.title: Accelerating Long-Tail Generation in Synchronous RLHF Training via Adaptive Tensor Parallelism
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new method called Adaptive Tensor Parallelism (PAT) to improve the efficiency of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) training. The traditional synchronous three-stage RLHF pipeline is often bottlenecked by the generation stage, which can lead to underutilized GPUs and long response times. PAT dynamically reconfigures tensor parallelism during the generation stage to optimize performance, reducing latency by up to 34.6% and end-to-end iteration latency by up to 27.2%.

results.seo_title: PALoRA: Preserving Reasoning in Large Language Models
results.meta_description: Efficiently updating LLMs with new knowledge while preserving reasoning abilities is a central challenge.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Efficiently updating Large Language Models (LLMs) with new or evolving factual knowledge remains a central challenge, as even parameter-efficient adaptation can erode previously acquired reasoning abilities.

PALoRA's two-stage framework for knowledge injection with reduced interference Singular Value Fine-Tuning (SVF) expert on a reasoning dataset Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) under a structural orthogonality constraint

ModelReasoning Performance Llama 3.1 8B95% of SVF expert's reasoning performance Mistral 7B95% of SVF expert's reasoning performance

Advantages over Prior Methods PALoRA consistently improves skill retention over prior spectral Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods while adding less than 0.006% parameter overhead.

source.title: PALoRA: Projection-Adaptive LoRA for Preserving Reasoning in Large Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new framework called PALoRA, which aims to efficiently update large language models with new knowledge without eroding their reasoning abilities. The framework uses a two-stage approach that identifies critical components of the model's skill-relevant subspace and updates them selectively. According to the study, PALoRA preserves on average 95% of the model's original reasoning performance while maintaining competitive factual recall. This is an improvement over existing methods in terms of skill retention with minimal added parameter overhead.

results.seo_title: LLMs and A* Search: Improving Efficiency in Natural Language Inference
results.meta_description: A new approach to natural language inference using LLMs and A* search, improving efficiency and accuracy.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Large language models (LLMs) can learn to generate correct and efficient proofs with guidance from A* search, an algorithm that guarantees an optimally efficient path to a goal.

Improving Accuracy with Simple Correctness Rewards

Our analysis uncovers a trade-off: while simple correctness rewards maximize accuracy, A*-informed signals strike a balance between accuracy and efficiency.

  • Simple correctness rewards maximize accuracy.
  • A*-informed signals strike a balance between accuracy and efficiency.

Efficiency Gains with Imperfect Heuristics

We find that on larger search spaces, models trained with imperfect heuristics exhibit superior accuracy.

  • Models trained with imperfect heuristics exhibit superior accuracy on larger search spaces.

Comparison to DeepSeek-V3.2

We outperform DeepSeek-V3.2, a much larger model, after A* post-training.

ModelAccuracy
Llama-3.2Near-zero accuracy to outperforming DeepSeek-V3.2

Conclusion and Future Work

Our results demonstrate a promising direction towards reasoning guided by principles derived from classical search algorithms.

Classical search algorithms offer a promising direction for improving efficiency in natural language inference.

source.title: Learning to Reason Efficiently with A* Post-Training
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new training technique for large language models (LLMs) to improve their deductive reasoning capabilities. The technique uses A* search algorithm to guide the LLMs in generating correct and efficient proofs, resulting in significant improvements in accuracy compared to existing models. The study found that smaller LLMs benefit substantially from this approach, while larger models may require more complex heuristics to achieve similar results. This new method has the potential to improve the reasoning capabilities of LLMs.

results.seo_title: Agentic Discovery of Neural Architectures: AIRA-Compose and…
results.meta_description: Researchers introduce a dual-framework approach to autonomously design foundation models beyond standard Transformers.
results.body_html:
AIRA-Compose and AIRA-Design introduce a dual-framework approach to autonomously design foundation models beyond standard Transformers.

Exploring Fundamental Computational Primitives

11 agents explore fundamental computational primitives under a 24-hour budget. Agents evaluate million-parameter candidates, extrapolating top designs to 350M, 1B, and 3B scales. This yields 14 architectures across two families: AIRAformers (Transformer-based) and AIRAhybrids (Transformer-Mamba).

AIRAformer-C scaling 54% AIRAhybrid-C scaling 71%

AIRAformer-C vs Llama 3.2 54% AIRAhybrid-C vs Nemotron-2 23%

AIRAformer-C vs Composer's best Transformer 54% AIRAhybrid-C vs Composer's best hybrid 37%

Autonomous Discovery of Architectures and Algorithmic Optimizations AI agents can autonomously discover architectures and algorithmic optimizations matching or surpassing hand-designed baselines.

AIRAformer-D improves accuracy by 2.4% over Llama 3.2 on downstream tasks. AIRAhybrid-D improves accuracy by 3.8% over Llama 3.2 on downstream tasks.

source.title: Agentic Discovery of Neural Architectures: AIRA-Compose and AIRA-Design
source.summary: Toward recursive self-improvement, we investigate LLM agents autonomously designing foundation models beyond standard Transformers. We introduce a dual-framework approach: AIRA-Compose for high-level architecture search, and AIRA-Design for low-level mechanistic implementation. AIRA-Compose uses 11 agents to explore fundamental computational primitives under a 24-hour budget. Agents evaluate million-parameter candidates, extrapolating top designs to 350M, 1B, and 3B scales. This yields 14 architectures across two families: AIRAformers (Transformer-based) and AIRAhybrids (Transformer-Mamba). Pre-trained at 1B scale, these consistently outperform Llama 3.2 and Composer-found baselines. On downstream tasks, AIRAformer-D and AIRAhybrid-D improve accuracy by 2.4% and 3.8% over Llama 3.2. Furthermore, AIRA-Compose finds models with highly efficient scaling frontiers: AIRAformer-C scales 54% and 71% faster than Llama 3.2 and Composer's best Transformer, while AIRAhybrid-C outscales Nemotron-2 by 23% and Composer's best hybrid by 37%. AIRA-Design tasks 20 agents with writing novel attention mechanisms for long-range dependencies and high-performing training scripts. On the Long Range Arena benchmark, agent-designed architectures reach within 2.3% and 2.6% of human state-of-the-art on document matching and text classification. On the Autoresearch benchmark, Greedy Opus 4.5 achieves 0.968 validation bits-per-byte under a fixed time budget, surpassing the published minimum. Together, these frameworks show AI agents can autonomously discover architectures and algorithmic optimizations matching or surpassing hand-designed baselines. This establishes a powerful paradigm for discovering next-generation foundation models, marking a clear step toward recursive self-improvement.

results.seo_title: Tokenizer Fertility and Zero-Shot Performance of Foundation Models…
results.meta_description: A comparative study on tokenizer fertility and zero-shot performance of foundation models on Ukrainian legal text, highlighting the importance of tokenizer…
results.body_html:
Tokenizer fertility varies 1.6x across foundation models on Ukrainian legal text.

Token Analysis is Crucial for Cost-Efficient Deployment

Qwen 3 models consume 60% more tokens than Llama-family models on identical input, making tokenizer analysis a prerequisite for cost-efficient deployment. The common tokenizer evaluation metric fertility is not always predictive of model downstream performance. Tokenizer choice can significantly impact the model's downstream performance and training costs.

NVIDIA Nemotron Super 3 Achieves Highest Composite Score

NVIDIA Nemotron Super 3 (120B) 83.1

Mistral Large 3 5.6x more total parameters, one-third API cost model scale

Few-Shot Prompting Degraded Performance by Up to 26 Percentage Points Few-shot prompting degrades performance by up to 26 percentage points; stratified and prompt-sensitivity ablations confirm this is intrinsic to Ukrainian-language demonstrations, not an artifact of example selection. Cross-Temporal Generalization Experiment Reveals Performance Gaps

ModelPerformance on Pre-War and Full-Scale Invasion Era Decisions

Newer Models +14.6 pp above forward transfer, but fail catastrophically on wartime legal language.

Older Models Lose 27.9 percentage points when applied to full-scale invasion era decisions.

source.title: Tokenizer Fertility and Zero-Shot Performance of Foundation Models on Ukrainian Legal Text: A Comparative Study
source.summary: Researchers conducted a comparative study on foundation models' performance on Ukrainian legal text, specifically focusing on tokenizer fertility and zero-shot performance. The study found significant variations in tokenizer consumption among different models, with some consuming up to 60% more tokens than others. Additionally, the researchers discovered that few-shot prompting can significantly degrade performance, particularly for morphologically rich languages like Ukrainian.

results.seo_title: FORGE: Self-Evolving Agent Memory With No Weight Updates via…
results.meta_description: FORGE proposes a staged, population-based protocol that evolves prompt-injected natural-language memory for hierarchical ReAct agents without gradient updates.
results.body_html:
Can LLM agents improve decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates? We propose FORGE (Failure-Optimized Reflective Graduation and Evolution), a staged, population-based protocol that evolves prompt-injected natural-language memory for hierarchical ReAct agents.

FORGE improves average evaluation return by 1.7-7.7× over zero-shot baseline and by 29-72% over Reflexion baseline in all 12 model-representation conditions. Population broadcast is critical mechanism, with a no-graduation ablation confirming that broadcast carries the performance gains while graduation primarily saves compute. Examples achieves the strongest returns for three of four models, Rules offers the best cost-reliability profile with ~40% fewer tokens.

Model Representation Condition Average Evaluation Return (x) Major-Failure Rates (<%)

Key Takeaways FORGE mitigates capability gaps rather than amplifying strong models, suggesting a potential solution for improving decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates.

source.title: FORGE: Self-Evolving Agent Memory With No Weight Updates via Population Broadcast
source.summary: Can LLM agents improve decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates? We propose FORGE (Failure-Optimized Reflective Graduation and Evolution), a staged, population-based protocol that evolves prompt-injected natural-language memory for hierarchical ReAct agents. FORGE wraps a Reflexion-style inner loop, where a dedicated reflection agent (using the same underlying LLM, no distillation from a stronger model) converts failed trajectories into reusable knowledge artifacts: textual heuristics (Rules), few-shot demonstrations (Examples), or both (Mixed), with an outer loop that propagates the best-performing instance's memory to the population between stages and freezes converged instances via a graduation criterion. We evaluate on CybORG CAGE-2, a stochastic network-defense POMDP at a 30-step horizon against the B-line attacker, where all four tested LLM families (Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Grok-4-Fast, Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-235B) exhibit strongly negative, heavy-tailed zero-shot rewards. Compared against both a zero-shot baseline and a Reflexion baseline (isolated single-stream learning), FORGE improves average evaluation return by 1.7-7.7$\times$ over zero-shot and by 29-72% over Reflexion in all 12 model-representation conditions, reducing major-failure rates (below $-100$) to as low as $\sim$1%. We find that (1) population broadcast is critical mechanism, with a no-graduation ablation confirming that broadcast carries the performance gains while graduation primarily saves compute; (2) Examples achieves the strongest returns for three of four models, Rules offers the best cost-reliability profile with $\sim$40% fewer tokens; and (3) weaker baseline models benefit disproportionately, suggesting FORGE may mitigate capability gaps rather than amplify strong models. All evidence is confined to CAGE-2 B-line; cross-family findings are directional evidence.

results.seo_title: Evaluating Large Language Models in Secret Hitler Game
results.meta_description: Researchers investigate LLMs' deceptive capabilities in a complex hidden role game, highlighting performance gaps and limitations.
results.body_html:
Quantifying the deceptive potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) is critical for AI safety, yet difficult to achieve in uncontrolled environments.

Models like Llama 3.1 70B achieve only a 59.7% accuracy in Secret Hitler games. Chain-of-Thought prompting nor internal memory bring improvements in performance, with up to 23.2% worse win rates for fascist roles. Rule-based agents align with expert human voting decisions 86.7% of the time.

Models playing as Fascists consistently yield negative impact scores and fail to sustain deception.

Roughly 40% shorter games compared to humans.

Research Framework and Metrics Role Identification Accuracy, Deception Retention Rate, and Game State Impact Rate are novel metrics introduced in this study.
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Evaluating Large Language Models in a Complex Hidden Role Game
source.summary: Researchers have evaluated the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) in a complex game called Secret Hitler, which requires strategic thinking and deception. They found that LLMs struggle with this task, even when compared to rule-based algorithms and human players, and that certain techniques like Chain-of-Thought prompting do not improve their performance. The study also identified that models playing as Fascists tend to lose games quickly due to their inability to sustain deception.

results.seo_title: Meta's LLMs Use Positional Number Copying to Dominate Arithmetic CoT…
results.meta_description: A new study reveals that Meta's large language models use a positional number copying shortcut to achieve high accuracy in arithmetic tasks, bypassing logical…
results.body_html:
The model copies whichever number occupies the trailing position before the answer delimiter, regardless of intermediate reasoning. This positional shortcut allows for high accuracy in arithmetic tasks, even when logical sequencing is not necessary.

Key FindingsGold-answer presence accounts for 54-92 pp of accuracy (89-92% of each model's teacher-forcing ceiling).The copy channel takes precedence over retained-context completion.Replacing the trailing number with a wrong value collapses accuracy to near-zero, while removing it recovers 5-32 pp above that floor.MethodologyMethodologyThree 1-3B instruction-tuned LMs on GSM8K were used to isolate the answer-readout stage via prefix completion.ConclusionThe positional number copying shortcut is a key factor in achieving high accuracy in arithmetic tasks, and it can be leveraged to improve the performance of large language models.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: The Readout Shortcut: Positional Number Copying Dominates Arithmetic CoT Readout in Small Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have found that in small language models, the "copy channel" plays a crucial role in arithmetic tasks, allowing the model to accurately read out answers even when it hasn't performed intermediate reasoning. The model's ability to copy the last number before the answer delimiter is responsible for 54-92% accuracy on gold-answer items, and this shortcut takes precedence over other completion strategies. This finding suggests that the model's architecture is using a positional shortcut to solve arithmetic problems, rather than relying solely on logical sequencing.

results.seo_title: Memorization Dynamics of Fill-in-the-Middle Pretraining
results.meta_description: Researchers study memorization dynamics of fill-in-the-middle pretraining in a controlled setting using Llama 3.2 models.
results.body_html:
Verbatim extraction under FIM-training grows approximately linearly with repetitions over the tested range.

FIM more often recovers short or partially matching spans, while LTR more often assigns high confidence to long exact continuations. Evaluating native FIM-format probes reveals that suffix context is not sufficient: verbatim recall under FIM-training remains strongly anchored in prefix context.

Repetitions and Verbatim Extraction

Repetition RangeVerbatim Extraction Growth ShortLinearly increases with repetitions. ModerateNo significant growth observed. LongNo significant growth observed.

Nuances in Memorization Behavior Our results also show that evaluating only one span length or probing format can miss important nuances in memorization behavior.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Memorization Dynamics of Fill-in-the-Middle Pretraining
source.summary: Researchers studied the memorization dynamics of Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) pretraining, a technique used to equip language models with infilling ability. They found that FIM-trained models more often recover short or partially matching spans than long exact continuations, and that verbatim extraction under FIM-training grows linearly with repetitions. The study also revealed that suffix context is not sufficient for verbatim recall under FIM-training, which relies heavily on prefix context.

results.seo_title: Brain-LLM Alignment Tracks Training Data, Not Typology
results.meta_description: Researchers find that training data composition drives brain-LLM alignment, not typological structure.
results.body_html:
Brain-LLM alignment does not generalize cross-linguistically, but is driven by training data composition.

Training-language dominance drives the alignment pattern, not an inherent property of English. A Chinese-dominant model aligns best with Chinese brains and worst with English. Training data composition is the primary factor governing the variation in brain-LLM alignment.

Typological Distance and Alignment Degradation

Formal Typological Distance Alignment Degradation

2.3 imes steeper typological gradients than lexico-semantic regions (PTL) 60% of a cross-linguistic shift in optimal encoding layer accounted for by tokenization fertility

Limitations and Future Work Further research is needed to understand the relationship between typological structure and brain-LLM alignment.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Brain-LLM Alignment Tracks Training Data, Not Typology
source.summary: Researchers have found that the alignment between language models and human brains is not universal across languages, contrary to previous assumptions about English's linguistic dominance. Instead, the alignment pattern is driven by the type of language used to train the model, with Chinese-dominant models showing a stronger correlation with brain activity than English-dominant models. The study also found that typological distance and syntax-related brain regions play a significant role in determining the strength of alignment.

results.seo_title: Sparse Autoencoders Map Brain-LLM Alignment onto Cortical Semantic…
results.meta_description: Researchers use sparse autoencoders to bridge mechanistic interpretability with neural encoding models, achieving robust findings in computational…
results.body_html:
Intermediate layers of large language models (LLMs) best predict human brain responses to language, one of the most robust findings in computational neurolinguistics.

Key Findings

  • A human-validated taxonomy reveals that semantic features alone recover 94% of peak encoding performance.
  • Sparse autoencoder-discovered features recapitulate known cortical semantic organization at a granularity inaccessible to prior methods.
  • Sparse autoencoders further predict human reading times beyond lexical controls.

Methodology

Feature Extraction Method Description
Sparse Autoencoders A biologically realistic n

Results and Convergence

Formal convergence test confirms this alignment (Spearman ρ=0.72, p<0.001; hypergeometric p=0.007).

Results generalize across English, Chinese, and French.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Sparse Autoencoders Map Brain-LLM Alignment onto Cortical Semantic Topography
source.summary: Researchers have used sparse autoencoders to analyze large language models (LLMs) and found that intermediate layers of these models can predict human brain responses to language with high accuracy. The study successfully mapped LLM features onto a cortical semantic topography, revealing distinct brain regions associated with specific semantic subcategories. This work demonstrates the potential for mechanistic interpretability in understanding how the brain processes language, and its findings generalize across multiple languages.

results.seo_title: DreamerNLplus: Interpretable Modeling of Mental Health Dynamics from…
results.meta_description: A hybrid framework for modeling mental health dynamics from social media timelines using Hybrid Rule-Based and RAG Methods.
results.body_html:
DreamerNLplus is a hybrid framework for modeling mental health dynamics from social media timelines using Hybrid Rule-Based and RAG Methods.

LLM-based data augmentation, DeBERTa classification, and Random Forest regression for structured state prediction Few-shot prompting with a locally deployed Llama 3.1 model to detect Switch and Escalation events using short-term temporal context Deterministic rule-based summarization pipeline and few-shot LLM-based approach for sequence-level summarization RAG-based method achieves strong performance in Task 3.2, ranking 1st for Improvement and 3rd for Deterioration

Task 1: Psychological State Modeling LLM-based data augmentation, DeBERTa classification, and Random Forest regression 2nd

Task 2: Temporal Change Detection Few-shot prompting with a locally deployed Llama 3.1 model N/A

Task 3.1: Sequence-Level Summarization Deterministic rule-based summarization pipeline and few-shot LLM-based approach 2nd

Task 3.2: Improvement RAG-based method 1st

Task 3.2: Deterioration RAG-based method 3rd

Challenges and Limitations Our analysis reveals key challenges, including the mismatch between classification and regression performance, the difficulty of modeling temporal transitions, and the disagreement between semantic and similarity-based evaluation metrics.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: DreamerNLplus: Interpretable Modeling of Mental Health Dynamics from Social Media Timelines using Hybrid Rule-Based and RAG Methods
source.summary: Researchers have developed a hybrid framework called DreamerNLplus to model mental health dynamics from social media timelines, addressing three tasks: psychological state modeling, temporal change detection, and sequence-level summarization. The system achieved strong performance in some tasks, including ranking 1st for Improvement and 3rd for Deterioration using a Recurrent Active Learning (RAG) method. However, it also highlighted challenges such as the mismatch between classification and regression performance and the difficulty of modeling temporal transitions. The code and prompts for DreamerNLplus are available on GitHub.

results.seo_title: Model Collapse as Cultural Evolution: Understanding LLM Degradation
results.meta_description: Researchers explore the concept of model collapse in large language models (LLMs) and its connection to cultural evolution, providing insights into the…
results.body_html:
Model collapse, the progressive degradation of LLMs trained on their own outputs, has been characterized statistically but lacks a linguistic explanation for which structures degrade, in what order, and why.

Iterated learning theory from cultural evolution fills the gap in understanding model collapse. Compositionality follows a non-monotonic trajectory under unfiltered self-training. The critical discriminative finding persists with maximally regular seed data and is sustained only by task-grounded filtering.

Predictions are confirmed with large effect sizes, and LLM regularization gradients closely match human behavioral data. Model collapse reframes as a cultural transmission phenomenon yielding concrete principles for self-training pipeline design.

LLM Self-Training Over 10 Generations LanguageHedges' gBF10 English2.3500 German2.1400 Turkish2.4600

Cultural Evolution and LLM Degradation Compositionality follows a non-monotonic trajectory under unfiltered self-training, persisting with maximally regular seed data. This signature is sustained only by task-grounded filtering, providing the first LLM-scale evidence for the compression-communication tradeoff.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Model Collapse as Cultural Evolution
source.summary: Researchers have used the theory of cultural evolution to explain the degradation of large language models (LLMs) over time, known as "model collapse". They found that compositionality, or the ability of LLMs to break down complex tasks into smaller components, follows a non-monotonic trajectory under self-training. The results suggest that model collapse is driven by a tradeoff between compression and communication in language models, with task-grounded filtering being necessary to prevent it. The findings provide new insights into how to design self-training pipelines for LLMs.

results.seo_title: LLM Fine-Tuning Evaluation: A JEPA Audit for Representation Without…
results.meta_description: A study on LLM fine-tuning evaluation using Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA), exploring the relationship between hidden-state representation and…
results.body_html:
Several auxiliaries clear single-cell paired α = 0.10 without correction, but none survives Bonferroni or Holm--Bonferroni at the relevant family-wise threshold.

JEPA Evaluation Metrics

  • T3-Local: Δ = +2.53~pp, p = 0.003 (strongest)
  • Fisher-metric Jacobi residuals
  • Decoder-visible JEPA objective

Robustness Across LoRA and Full Fine-Tuning

The null is robust across LoRA and full fine-tuning for the decoder-visible construction.

Coupling Problem in LLM-Domain JEPA Evaluation

Hidden-state representation work and decoded-task accuracy are therefore weakly coupled in this regime; we accordingly reframe LLM-domain JEPA evaluation as a coupling problem, in which the operative question is under which metrics useful hidden geometry becomes decoder-visible task signal.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Representation Without Reward: A JEPA Audit for LLM Fine-Tuning
source.summary: Joint-embedding predictive architectures (JEPAs) propose that a model should learn more useful abstractions when trained to predict latent representations rather than observed outputs. For autoregressive language-model fine-tuning the principle entails a stricter requirement: the induced hidden-state geometry must reach the language-model head \emph{and} improve the decoded task metric. We test that requirement under a fixed Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct LoRA harness on natural-language-to-regex generation, comparing twenty-two training-time auxiliaries across trajectory-shape regularisation, distributional constraints, predictor/target asymmetry, Fisher-metric Jacobi residuals, and a decoder-visible JEPA objective constructed to lie in cross-entropy's positive cone. The empirical answer is a structured null: several auxiliaries clear single-cell paired $\alpha = 0.10$ without correction (T3-Local at $\Delta = +2.53$~pp, $p = 0.003$ being the strongest), but none survives Bonferroni or Holm--Bonferroni at the relevant family-wise threshold, even though many change curvature, anisotropy, variance, and gradient direction. Decoder-visible JEPA yields the first positive auxiliary--cross-entropy gradient cosine in the study, yet exact match remains inside seed noise; a full-fine-tuning replication of the same auxiliary at $n = 5$ seeds reproduces the null on both benchmarks (TURK: $\Delta = +0.04$~pp, $p_{\text{paired}} = 0.96$; SYNTH: $\Delta = +0.52$~pp, $p_{\text{paired}} = 0.28$), so the null is robust across LoRA and full fine-tuning for the decoder-visible construction. Hidden-state representation work and decoded-task accuracy are therefore weakly coupled in this regime; we accordingly reframe LLM-domain JEPA evaluation as a coupling problem, in which the operative question is under which metrics useful hidden geometry becomes decoder-visible task signal.

results.seo_title: Improving Noisy Embedding Techniques in Instruction Finetuning
results.meta_description: A new fine-tuning method for language models utilizing symmetric noise in embeddings improves performance over state-of-the-art methods.
results.body_html:
Our approach, SymNoise, increases the score significantly to 69.04%, using symmetric noisy embeddings.

Comparing Noise Types

  • Uniform noise outperforms Gaussian noise in NEFTune's empirical findings.
  • Comparable performance among these noise types is indicated by our thorough analysis.
  • Gaussian noise does not surpass uniform noise in terms of performance.

Theoretical Analysis

Our approach aims to enhance the model's function by more stringently regulating its local curvature.

Key MetricDescription
Local Curvature RegulationStringent regulation of local curvature to enhance model performance.
Performance ImprovementA 6.7% improvement over the state-of-the-art method, NEFTune.

Empirical Results

SymNoise consistently outperforms NEFTune on various models and stronger baseline instruction datasets.

  • Evol-Instruct
  • ShareGPT
  • OpenPlatypus
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Understanding and Improving Noisy Embedding Techniques in Instruction Finetuning
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new technique called SymNoise to improve noisy embedding techniques in instruction finetuning for language models. The study found that using symmetric noise in embeddings outperforms uniform noise and significantly improves performance on benchmark datasets, with an average increase of 6.7% over the current state-of-the-art method, NEFTune. This new approach demonstrates superior performance on various models and stronger baseline instruction datasets, providing a significant step forward in understanding and improving noisy embedding techniques.

results.seo_title: Is Capability a Liability? More Capable Language Models Make Worse For
results.meta_description: We document inverse scaling in LLMs on forecasting problems whose underlying time series exhibit superlinear growth and tail risk of regime change, a…
results.body_html:
We document inverse scaling in LLMs on forecasting problems whose underlying time series exhibit superlinear growth and tail risk of regime change, a structure common in finance and epidemiology. On these tasks, more capable models produce worse distributional forecasts. The pattern appears on ForecastBench-Sim (FBSim), a contamination-free, simulated-world benchmark we release, in forecasting synthetic SIR epidemics with a matched linear control, and replicates in real-world datasets on COVID-19, measles, housing markets, and hyperinflation.

A per-quantile decomposition shows the failure concentrates at the upper tail, which more capable models shift upward to track aggressive extrapolations of growth, while the lower tail stays put. A within-family study of Llama-3.1 shows that both model scale and post-training independently contribute to this effect. Domain knowledge does not reliably rescue calibration.

This inverse scaling does not appear on single-threshold metrics common in LLM forecasting benchmarks, reversing the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on identical outputs. Single-threshold scoring at conventional cutoffs misses the upper-tail cost; tail-inclusive scoring reverses the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on the same outputs. We recommend that LLM forecasting evaluations use continuous (and unbounded) measures of accuracy alongside bounded binary threshold metrics.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Is Capability a Liability? More Capable Language Models Make Worse Forecasts When It Matters Most
source.summary: Researchers have found that more capable language models (LLMs) perform worse in forecasting problems where time series exhibit superlinear growth and tail risk, such as in finance and epidemiology. This phenomenon, known as inverse scaling, occurs because more capable models tend to overestimate the upper tail of forecasts, leading to poorer performance. The issue was tested on both simulated and real-world datasets, including COVID-19 and housing markets data. The study recommends using continuous accuracy measures alongside traditional binary threshold metrics for evaluating LLM forecasting performance.

results.seo_title: Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models for Schema-Constrained…
results.meta_description: Researchers propose a modular retrieval-augmented generation pipeline to improve clinical information extraction from conversational nurse-patient transcripts.
results.body_html:
Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models (RAG) improve performance in clinical information extraction from conversational nurse-patient transcripts by up to 80.36% F1 score.

Modular RAG PipelineUses training set as exemplar corpusCombines schema-constrained prompting (full schema vs. pruned candidate schema)Deterministic schema-based postprocessingA second-pass audit for residual schema-adherence errorsLLM Backbones and Embedding ModelsLLM BackboneEmbedding ModelGPT-5.2 with full schema, RAG, and second-pass auditingCorresponding embedding models for RAGOptimal Degree of Schema ConstraintThe optimal degree of schema constraint depends on the model, with GPT-5.2 achieving the best results.

source.title: Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models for Schema-Constrained Clinical Information Extraction
source.summary: Conversational nurse-patient transcripts contain actionable observations, but converting these transcripts into structured representations at scale remains challenging. Documentation burden is substantial, with prior studies showing clinicians spend large portions of their workday on documentation and related desk work rather than direct patient care. MEDIQA-SYNUR focuses on observation extraction from conversational nurse-patient transcripts, requiring systems to normalize these narratives into a predefined schema with value-type constraints. We propose a modular retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline that uses the training set as an exemplar corpus, combines schema-constrained prompting (full schema vs. pruned candidate schema), deterministic schema-based postprocessing, and a second-pass audit, with two LLM backbones: Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct and GPT-5.2 with corresponding embedding models for RAG. Our best configuration uses GPT-5.2 with full schema, RAG, and a second-pass auditing, achieving 80.36% F1 score. Overall, our results show that RAG consistently improves performance, while the optimal degree of schema constraint depends on the model, and second-pass auditing yields modest additional gains by correcting residual schema-adherence errors.
